content.problemStatement:
You are given positive integers NNN and KKK.

You have to construct an array AAA of length NNN such that :


1≤Ai≤10181 \leq A_i \leq 10^{18}1≤Ai​≤1018
∑i=1N∑j=iNF(i,j)=K\sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=i}^{N} F(i,j) = K∑i=1N​∑j=iN​F(i,j)=K, where F(i,j)F(i,j)F(i,j) denotes the gcd of all elements of the subarray A[i,j]A[i, j]A[i,j].
If multiple such arrays exist, print any.

Report −1-1−1 if no such array exists.

Note that A[l,r]A[l, r]A[l,r] denotes the subarray [Al,Al+1,…,Ar−1,Ar][A_l ,A_{l+1}, \ldots, A_{r-1}, A_r][Al​,Al+1​,…,Ar−1​,Ar​].


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of single line of input.

The only line of each test case contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK — the number of elements and required sum.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line NNN space-separated integers, denoting array AAA.

Report −1-1−1 if no such array exists.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 1000.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains two space‑separated integers N and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output a single line. If it is impossible to construct such an array, output -1. Otherwise output N space‑separated integers A1 … AN (1 ≤ Ai ≤ 10^18) that satisfy the required sum.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: N=1, base=1, K=5 → first=5, array [5].\nTest case 2: N=2, base=3, K=4 → first=2, array [2,1]; subarray gcds are 2,1,1 → sum=4.\nTest case 3: N=3, base=6, K=1 < 6 → impossible, output -1.

hidden.label: edge case where K equals minimal sum

solution.approach:
For any array, the sum of gcds of all subarrays is at least the number of subarrays, because each subarray contributes at least 1. The number of subarrays of length N is base = N·(N+1)/2. If we set every element to 1, every subarray has gcd = 1 and the total sum equals base. To increase the sum we can make a single element larger. Choose A1 = X (1 ≤ X ≤ 10^18) and set all other elements to 1. All subarrays that do not consist solely of A1 still have gcd = 1, while the subarray [1,1] has gcd = X. Hence the total sum becomes base + (X‑1). By picking X = K‑base+1 we obtain exactly K, provided K ≥ base and X ≤ 10^18. Therefore:
1. Compute base = N·(N+1)/2.
2. If K < base → impossible, output -1.
3. Otherwise set A1 = K‑base+1 and Ai = 1 for i > 1.
The construction always respects the limits because base ≤ 500 500 (N ≤ 1000) and K ≤ 10^18, so A1 ≤ 10^18.
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case to output the array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case (dominated by printing the N numbers).
